name: The New Family
text:
The family that takes you in has a door that closes properly.

This is the first thing you notice. Not the food, not the bed, not the woman who smiles too much or the man who keeps his distance. The door. It has a latch. It stays shut when you shut it. In the camp, nothing closed properly. Tents don't have doors. Fences have gaps. Walls have ears.

The room they give you is small and has a window. You sleep under the window for three nights before you can sleep in the bed. The bed is too soft. The softness feels like a trick.

They have a son. He's a year younger than you and he has never been hungry and he doesn't know that about himself. You can tell by the way he leaves food on his plate. Half a potato. A crust. Things you would have fought for six months ago. You don't say anything. You eat what's on your plate and you don't look at what's on his.

The woman — your new mother, though neither of you uses that word yet — notices. She starts giving you slightly more. She does it without comment, the way Maret used to put her hand on your back. Quiet. Deliberate. A kindness that knows better than to announce itself.

You live with them for two years before you stop checking the door at night.
